questions.question: Explain how shadows are formed.
questions.answer: Shadows are formed when an opaque object blocks the path of light, preventing it from reaching a surface behind the object.

questions.question: Describe the difference between a transparent, translucent, and opaque object.
questions.answer: Transparent objects allow light to pass through completely, translucent objects allow partial light, and opaque objects block light entirely.

questions.question: Explain why the size of a shadow changes during the day.
questions.answer: The size of a shadow changes because the position of the light source (like the Sun) changes, altering the angle at which light falls on the object.

questions.question: How does a pinhole camera work?
questions.answer: A pinhole camera works by allowing light from an object to pass through a small hole and project an inverted image on the opposite side of a darkened box.

questions.question: Explain the difference between regular and diffused reflection.
questions.answer: Regular reflection occurs on smooth surfaces and produces clear images, while diffused reflection occurs on rough surfaces and scatters light in many directions, producing no clear image.

questions.question: Why do we see ourselves in a plane mirror?
questions.answer: We see ourselves in a plane mirror because light from our body reflects off the mirror in a predictable direction, forming an image that appears behind the mirror.

questions.question: Describe how periscopes help submarines see above water.
questions.answer: Periscopes use two plane mirrors inclined at 45° to reflect light from above the water down into the viewer’s eye, allowing observation without exposing the submarine.

questions.question: Explain why shadows are darker at noon compared to early morning or late afternoon.
questions.answer: Shadows are darker at noon because the Sun is directly overhead, so light is less scattered and more concentrated around the object.

questions.question: What is the principle behind the formation of an image by a plane mirror?
questions.answer: The principle is that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, so light rays reflect in a predictable manner to form a virtual image.

questions.question: Explain why the length of a shadow changes with the height of the object and distance from light.
questions.answer: A taller object casts a longer shadow if the light source is low, and increasing the distance of the object from the light source changes the shadow's length due to the changing angle of light incidence.

questions.question: Describe how concave mirrors can form real and virtual images.
questions.answer: Concave mirrors converge light. If the object is beyond the focal point, a real image is formed in front of the mirror. If the object is within the focal point, a virtual image appears behind the mirror.

questions.question: How do convex mirrors provide a wider field of view?
questions.answer: Convex mirrors diverge light rays, producing smaller images that cover a larger area, allowing the viewer to see more than with a plane mirror.

questions.question: Explain why shadows have the same shape as the object.
questions.answer: Shadows have the same shape as the object because they are formed by light being blocked by the object’s exact outline.

questions.question: Describe how images in a plane mirror are laterally inverted.
questions.answer: Images in a plane mirror are laterally inverted because the left and right sides of the object are reversed in the reflection, although the top and bottom remain unchanged.

questions.question: Explain why a pinhole camera produces an inverted image.
questions.answer: The pinhole allows light rays from the top of the object to reach the bottom of the screen and vice versa, producing an inverted image.

questions.question: Why does the size of the shadow decrease as the object moves away from the light source?
questions.answer: As the object moves away, the angle at which light rays are blocked becomes smaller, causing a smaller shadow on the surface.

questions.question: Explain why we cannot see through opaque objects.
questions.answer: Opaque objects do not allow light to pass through, so we cannot see objects on the other side.

questions.question: Describe how light enables us to see objects.
questions.answer: We see objects when light from a source reflects off the object and enters our eyes, allowing the brain to form an image.

questions.question: Explain why images in a convex mirror are always virtual and diminished.
questions.answer: Convex mirrors diverge light rays, so the reflected rays appear to come from behind the mirror, forming a virtual, smaller image.

questions.question: How does a sundial use shadows to tell time?
questions.answer: A sundial uses the position of the Sun and the shadow of its gnomon to indicate time based on the shadow’s direction and length.

questions.question: Explain why a rough surface does not form a clear reflection.
questions.answer: A rough surface scatters light in many directions, preventing a clear image from forming.

questions.question: Why is the angle of incidence equal to the angle of reflection?
questions.answer: This is a fundamental law of reflection, which states that when a light ray strikes a smooth surface, the angle at which it hits equals the angle at which it bounces off.

questions.question: Explain how concave mirrors are used in shaving or makeup mirrors.
questions.answer: Concave mirrors magnify the reflection of objects placed close to the mirror, making it easier to see details.

questions.question: Describe the effect of moving the light source closer to an object on the shadow.
questions.answer: Moving the light source closer makes the shadow larger and sharper because the light spreads out less before hitting the object.

questions.question: Explain why multiple shadows are formed when more than one light source is present.
questions.answer: Each light source produces its own shadow of the object. When multiple sources are present, multiple overlapping shadows are formed.
